coredata.dc:title: Catalase-like quantum dots of l-lysine polymerization as free radical scavengers for hypoxic brain injury
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qifeng
Gao, Yalong
Shen, Jun
Mu, Xiaoyu
Wang, Junying
Ouyang, Lufei
Chen, Ke
He, Hua
Pei, Jiahui
Ren, Qinjuan
Sun, Si
Liu, Haile
Zhou, Lei
Sun, Yuanming
Long, Wei
Zhang, Jianning
Zhang, Xiao-Dong
coredata.dc:description:
Hypoxemia as one of the most common symptoms of traumatic brain injury (TBI) can significantly exacerbate the development of secondary damage, which further contributes to generating large numbers of neurotoxic factors such as reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS), pro-inflammatory cytokine and chemokine. Here, we developed a kind of quantum dots as catalase-mimic and multiple free radical scavengers by means of the microwave heating polymerization of l-lysine. Hydrogen peroxide as overproduced radicals can be decomposed to supply oxygen, contributing to improving the hypoxic condition in brain. Simultaneously, other traditional RONS such as O2
−,
OH, NO and ONOO− can be eliminated by the quantum dots. The total ROS level is back to normal after the quantum dots treatment, promoting the H2O2/LPS-stimulated cell survival in vitro. Oxidative stress induced by hypoxic brain injury is restored by the quantum dots intravenous injection, leading to the protection of neural cells in vivo. Additionally, the quantum dots can improve the spatial learning and memory of TBI mice by water maze test. This work provides a new perspective for the therapy of hypoxic brain injury.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Traumatic brain injury
Hypoxemia
Catalase-mimic
Reactive oxygen and nitrogen species

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of catalase-like activity of L-QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Material properties of L-QDs (a) Schematic diagram of L-QDs synthesis process. (b) Hydrodynamic size of L-QDs. (c) FTIR spectra of lysine and L-QDs. (d) Fine XPS spectra of L-QDs for C 1s state. (e) Fine XPS spectra of L-QDs for N 1s state.

CAT-like activity of L-QDs. (a) Images of systems reacting for 30 min. (b) Absorbance of H2O2 at 240 nm with or without L-QDs. (c) Michaelis-Menten plot and (d) corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot of CAT-like activity of L-QDs.

RONS scavenging activities of L-QDs. (a)
OH, (b) O2
− (c)
NO and (d) ONOO− scavenging activities of L-QDs, respectively.

In vitro cell viability and ROS scavenging activity with the treatment of L-QDs, H2O2 and LPS. (a) Cell viability incubating with L-QDs for 24 h. (b) Cell survival after treated with 200 μM H2O2 that mixed with L-QDs. (c) Cell survival after treated with 0.5 mg/mL LPS that mixed with L-QDs. (d, f) Fluorescent images of general ROS without treatment, with H2O2 or LPS treatment and with L-QDs treatment and (e, g) corresponding results quantified by flow cytometer, respectively. (*p < 0.05 compared with control group, one-way ANOVA).

In vivo treatment of L-QDs to TBI mice. (a–d) Oxidative stress, including lipid peroxidation amount, H2O2 amount, SOD activity and GSH/GSSG amount of TBI mice with or without L-QDs treatment. a–c are normalized. (e) Apoptosis in brain tissue after TBI and (j) corresponding statistical results by fluorescence. (f, k) MMP-9 level, (g, l) neuron number, (h, m) astrocytes and (i, n) microglia activation level in brains, and corresponding statistical results by fluorescence, respectively. j–n are normalized. (*p < 0.05 compared with control group, one-way ANOVA).

Behavioral tests after TBI by morris water maze. (a) The latency between different groups (***P < 0.001 vs the control group; ##P < 0.01 vs TBI group; n = 10 each), (b) The swimming speed between different groups (n = 10 each), (c) The distance between different groups (***P < 0.001 vs the control group; ##P < 0.01 vs TBI group; n = 10 each), (d–f) Representative swimming trajectory heat maps of the mice in all three groups on the fifth day of the Morris water maze.

head.ce:title: Catalase-like quantum dots of l-lysine polymerization as free radical scavengers for hypoxic brain injury

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Hypoxemia as one of the most common symptoms of traumatic brain injury (TBI) can significantly exacerbate the development of secondary damage, which further contributes to generating large numbers of neurotoxic factors such as reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS), pro-inflammatory cytokine and chemokine. Here, we developed a kind of quantum dots as catalase-mimic and multiple free radical scavengers by means of the microwave heating polymerization of l-lysine. Hydrogen peroxide as overproduced radicals can be decomposed to supply oxygen, contributing to improving the hypoxic condition in brain. Simultaneously, other traditional RONS such as O2
−,
OH, NO and ONOO− can be eliminated by the quantum dots. The total ROS level is back to normal after the quantum dots treatment, promoting the H2O2/LPS-stimulated cell survival in vitro. Oxidative stress induced by hypoxic brain injury is restored by the quantum dots intravenous injection, leading to the protection of neural cells in vivo. Additionally, the quantum dots can improve the spatial learning and memory of TBI mice by water maze test. This work provides a new perspective for the therapy of hypoxic brain injury.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute brain injury from trauma as the “silent epidemic” has rapidly grown into an international health concern, which is complicated on account of the diversity in epidemiological types, injury patterns, and severity of traumatic brain injury (TBI) incidence [1,2]. Generally, the severity of TBI has been classiﬁed as mild, moderate and severe by a universal classiﬁcation system (Glasgow coma scale, GCS) [2,3]. The assessment score of GCS is constitutive of three components (motor response, eye opening and verbal scales). Severe TBI exhibits a significant likelihood of hypoxemia, hypotension and brain swelling [2–4] and has been linked to post-traumatic stress disorder, chronic neuroinflammation, neurodegenerative diseases and memory deficits [5]. Especially, hypoxemia as one of the most common symptoms can significantly exacerbate the development of secondary brain injury, which further increases the possibility of exanimation and even death [6–8]. However, its pathophysiological mechanism is complicated and multifarious. Therefore, how to improve the hypoxemia circumstance is a critical solution to relieve secondary brain injury.

The exacerbated secondary damage by hypoxia could continue for a long time in brain and stimulate mitochondria and NADPH oxidase (NOX) to generate a large number of neurotoxic factors such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) [9], as well as inflammatory cytokine and chemokine [10–12]. Importantly, ROS and RNS including hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide anion radicals (O2
−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), nitric oxide (NO) and peroxynitrite (ONOO−), play a key factor in the development of TBI, because these active radicals are strongly associated with free radical-induced oxidative stress. The overproduction of the radicals could react with protein, lipid and DNA, leading to dysfunction, lipid peroxidation and even neural cell apoptosis. When neural cells are being in the process of apoptosis, they would in turn increase the release of neurotoxic factors and pro-inflammatory cytokines, which could further extend the local inflammatory responses in brain [4,13,14]. Consequently, one of the most available paths to recover from brain injury is to effectively scavenge active free radicals. Up to now, it has been widely evaluated and well demonstrated that antioxidants and catalytic scavengers for free radicals including a series of inorganic and organic nanomaterials as well as macromolecules could protect against neuroinflammation and dramatically improve cerebral damage [15–40]. However, scarce control over the primal source of ROS generation, hypoxemia, is still an existing and hard problem to be solved. Importantly, simultaneous oxygen supply and free radical scavenging are of crucial importance and high urgency to improve hypoxic brain injury.

In this work, a catalase-like quantum dots polymerized by l-lysine (L-QDs) was developed using a facile microwave-heating way. L-QDs can supply oxygen via hydrogen peroxide decomposition, and simultaneously functions as multiple free radical scavengers. H2O2/LPS-stimulated Cell survival is effectively promoted in vitro, and oxidative stress and inflammatory responses are reduced in vivo after L-QDs treatment. In addition, motor function and spatial memory of TBI mice also show an improvement to a certain extent after intravenous injection of the L-QDs.

The synthesis route and structure of L-QDs are shown in Fig. 1
a. Fig. 1 b shows that the mean hydrodynamic size of the L-QDs is about 2.4 nm, which is less than the renal clearance cutoff 5.5 nm [41,42], proving a good water solubility and biosafety of L-QDs. Moreover, the mean size of L-QDs is 1.07 ± 0.37 nm by TEM analysis (Fig. S1). In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of L-QDs (Fig. S2), the absorption peak at 321 nm is derived from the π-π* transition of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 system [43]. In the fluorescence spectrum of L-QDs, the emission at 416 nm (excitation at 355 nm) is due to the group in the L-QDs, which produce a non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs through the passivation of the carboxyl group [44]. The fluorescent quantum yield was thus calculated to be ∼25 % using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. S3). For FTIR spectrum of L-QDs (Fig. 1c), the peak at 3377-3317 cm−1 can be attributed to the plane stretching vibration of OH and NH. The peak at 3085-3055 cm-1 indicates the presence of unsaturated = C–H in the L-QDs, while two peaks at 2947 cm-1 and 2870 cm-1 are the in-plane stretching vibrations corresponding to C–H. Further, it can be found that the peaks at 1566 cm−1 and 1488 cm−1 correspond to the in-plane stretching vibration of CC or NH−CO and the out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H, respectively. Therefore, some basic molecular structures of L-QDs have been observed, and more importantly, functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl amino groups have been deduced on the surface of L-QDs. The XPS full spectrum of L-QDs is shown in Fig. S4a. L-QDs contain three elements of C, N and O, and their proportion are 69.4 %, 17.0 % and 13.6 %, respectively. In Fig. 1d, the high-resolution C1 s spectrum of L-QDs is well fitting with several subpeaks, including the OC = O bond of carboxyl carbon at 288.1 eV, the CN bond of amino carbon at 287.5 eV, the CO of hydroxyl carbon at 286.2 eV, and the CC in the center of the structure at 284.6 eV [45]. Similarly, the bonding of oxygen elements is shown in Fig. S4b. In addition to carboxyl oxygen and hydroxyl oxygen, the presence of CO at 531.2 eV is also found. Furthermore, the high-resolution N1 s spectrum of L-QDs (Fig. 1e) displays that L-QDs possess pyridinic N at 398.8 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.8 eV and graphitic N at 400.9 eV [46]. These results indicate the bonding and condensation reactions during the formation of L-QDs.

The CAT-like activity of L-QDs is studied in Fig. 2 . The observable bubbles show up in the Fig. 2 a. Then, catalytic reaction of hydrogen peroxide by L-QDs over time is detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Fig. 2b). In the control group without L-QDs, the absorption value of hydrogen peroxide at 240 nm dose not decrease within 20 min. When L-QDs are present in the reaction solution, the absorption value of hydrogen peroxide at 240 nm gradually decreases with time, indicating that L-QDs could catalyze hydrogen peroxide to oxygen. The catalytic capacity of L-QDs is determined using the fluorescence dissolved oxygen analyzer by monitoring the increase of solubility of oxygen. In the presence of L-QDs, the solubility of oxygen increases, indicating that L-QDs exhibit a catalase-mimic. Steady-state kinetics are also studied to evaluate the catalytic properties of L-QDs at different concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (0−1500 mM). When the concentration of L-QDs is fixed at 4 μg/mL, the reaction rate increases with the increase of the concentration of hydrogen peroxide, and finally becomes saturated (Fig. 2c). The steady-state dynamics parameters are given in the Fig. 2 d following typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics, i.e. 432.72 mM of K m and 80.65 μM/min of V max.

In order to realize the ability of L-QDs to scavenge free radicals, we use different methods for evaluation. As shown in Fig. 3
a–c, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technology is used to demonstrate the scavenging ability of L-QDs for reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS) such as
OH, O2
−, and
NO. For
OH radicals (Fig. 3a), the scavenging ability of L-QDs under the different concentrations is studied in the reaction system. The attenuation of BMPO/
OH signal indicates that L-QDs exhibit as a
OH scavenger. The scavenging ability of L-QDs to O2
−, an important ROS, is investigated at 100 μg/mL and 600 μg/mL. It is found that the DEPMPO/
OOH signal is weakened to varying degrees, which reveals the L-QDs is able to remove O2
− (Fig. 3b). As shown in Fig. 3c, Carboxy-PTIO is the
NO capture agent that produces five ESR signals and SNAP is the generator of
NO. When SNAP is added to Carboxy-PTIO, the resulting
NO could reduce Carboxy-PTIO to Carboxy-PTI, leading to seven ESR signals. When L-QDs is added, the seven ESR signals are changed back to five, indicating that L-QDs could effectively remove the
NO. Next, we test the ability of L-QDs to remove ONOO−. We use the steady-state kinetic method to detect the change in the absorption value of ONOO− at 302 nm, and calculate the change of the clearance rate of ONOO− by L-QDs over time (Figs. 3 d and S5). After 90 min of reaction, the removal efficiency can reach 84.40 %, showing the excellent ability of L-QDs to remove ONOO−. To further reveal the total antioxidant ability of L-QDs to scavenge RONS, DPPH is used for evaluation. Fig. S6 shows that the scavenging capacity of DPPH linearly increases with the concentration of L-QDs until 50 μg/mL. Considering a 30 % radical scavenging activity, a solution of L-QDs at 37.2 μg/mL exhibits the same radical scavenging activity than a Trolox solution at 15.39 μM. Therefore, L-QDs possess CAT-like activity as well as multiple free radical scavenging capacities, which reveals the potential for hypoxia damage therapy.

To investigate applications of L-QDs in biomedicine, cell experiments in vitro are performed. First, cellular viability are tested using MTT assay, which shows extremely low cytotoxicity with dose up to 0.3 mg/mL (Fig. 4 a). Next cytoprotective function of L-QDs against H2O2 and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) are assessed in this work. After L-QDs treatment, survival rate of H2O2 groups increased from 76 % to healthy levels, while LPS groups also raise from 75 % to normal levels (Fig. 4 b and c). Cell survival in groups irritated by H2O2 and LPS shows good cell activity in a dose-dependent manner, which is due to high catalytic properties of L-QDs. Then to evaluate the ability of scavenging free radicals, fluorescent dye DCFH-DA are utilized to detect ROS levels. DCFH-DA can be oxidized by ROS to form green fluorescence products, while the nuclei labeled by Hoechst 33342 appear blue under fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 4 d–g, intercellular ROS levels significantly increase when cells are exposed to H2O2 or LPS irritation, indicating the cellular oxidative stress results from the stimuli [1–4]. After L-QDs treatment, ROS levels return to nearly normal level. Cellular uptake experiments further show that L-QDs can be engulfed into the cells to eliminate the intracellular ROS (Fig. S7). In vitro experiments prove that L-QDs with RONS scavenging properties are effective to reduce the damages induced by oxidative stress.

Furthermore, in vivo effects of L-QDs are performed in TBI mouse models. Oxidative stress-related indicators are investigated in TBI mice after L-QDs treatment. As shown in Fig. 5
a–d, due to oxidative stress and activation of immune cells, it is found that TBI could cause adverse lipid peroxidation, the H2O2 accumulation and the reduction of SOD and GSH/GSSG activities at two time points on the first and seventh days. In Fig. 5 a of the lipid peroxidation level, it is found that the lipid peroxidation is aggravated with the passage of time, and the treatment with L-QDs can inhibit the development of lipid peroxidation and promote the recovery of the dynamic balance of lipid peroxidation in cells effectively. In Fig. 5 b, the activity of SOD in the TBI group is significantly decreased, indicating that the antioxidant capacity of the body is decreased, while the activity of SOD in the TBI + L-QDs group is relatively increased. TBI causes excessive accumulation of hydrogen peroxide, resulting in oxidative stress that can cause damage to cells, while L-QDs can catalyze H2O2 and play a role in scavenging the free radicals (Fig. 5c). And the oxygen produced improves the state of hypoxia in the brain of TBI mice. The decreased GSH/GSSH ratio in Fig. 5 d indicates the increased oxygen partial pressure, and the ratio of L-QDs treatment group is higher than that of TBI group, indicating that L-QDs can further improve the activity of endogenous enzymes and promote the return of GSH and GSSG content to the normal level. Brain injury can induce membrane disruption, cell apoptosis and inflammatory reaction [5], which are demonstrated by immunofluorescent staining as shown in Fig. 5 e–n. Cell apoptosis is indeed severe after injury and gradually recover with L-QDs treatment, suggesting that L-QDs could prevent cell apoptosis (Fig. 5 e and j). Matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) is related with BBB opening and pathophysiology of TBI [6]. Therefore, MMP-9 level is invested in the brain injury. MMP-9 level (green staining) is overexpressed after injury and suppressed by treatment (Fig. 5 f and k). A large number of neurons can be seen in the normal group, but a large number of neurons are reduced in the TBI group. On the contrary, there is a significant increase in the group treated with L-QDs (Fig. 5 g and l). Astrocytes and microglia are key cellular mediators concerned with inflammasome [7]. After injury, the number of nerve cells in mice can be affected. The astrocytes and microglia around the damaged area are activated. In Fig. 5 h and i, a large amount of pro-inflammatory factors in the damaged area can be found. However, after L-QDs treatment, the morphology, number and biological function of the two types of cells are similar to that of normal cells. The good therapeutic effect of L-QDs is also confirmed by quantitative analysis of inflammatory inflammation as shown in Fig. 5 m and n. Therefore, L-QDs can mediate oxidative stress and inflammatory responses, thus inhibiting cell membrane destruction and cell death.

The spatial learning and memory recovery of mice are tested by Morris water maze test14 days after TBI. The result of the latency (P < 0.001) and swimming distance (P < 0.01) of the TBI mice were significantly increased compared with the control group at the fifth day (Fig. 6
a, c) and showed obvious learning and cognitive defects after TBI. In addition, compared with the TBI group, the latency and swimming distance of the TBI + L-QDs group were significantly attenuated. Furthermore, there is no significantly difference between the three groups in swimming speed (Fig. 6b). As can be seen from the track heat map of mice in Fig. 6 d–f, the activity areas of mice in the control group and the TBI + L-QDs group are mainly concentrated on the platform and its vicinity, while the mice in the TBI group move in the whole round pool and stay on the platform for a very short time, indicating that the L-QDs treatment could restore the spatial learning and memory ability of mice. The results suggest that L-QDs inhibit secondary injury via CAT-like and RONS scavenging abilities, and further ameliorate hippocampus damages.

As a common disease, TBI threatens human life and health. At present, with the continuous development and innovation of science and technology, many methods and technologies have been provided for the research and treatment of TBI, and researchers are also in a deeper understanding of its pathogenesis. The mechanism of brain microcirculation-induced hypoxia, free radical clearance and neuroinflammation in TBI have attracted much attention [47–49]. To restrict that secondary injury of TBI, efficient radical clearance regimens are highly desired. Carbon or graphene-based QDs are good antioxidant candidate owing to the low cytotoxicity, fast and large electron transfer and capacity. Besides, carbon series nanomaterials are characterized by their great flexibility for synthesis and modification, which contribute to their tunable functionalities. For example, EL-Hnayin et al. [50] used 2,2’-(Ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) and H2O2 to breakdown the graphene oxide, leaving the diamine at the surface to produce functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The as-synthesized quantum dots showed chelating abilities for Fe(II) and Cu(II), preventing the ROS-generative Fenton and Haber Weiss reaction in biology conditions and scavenge radicals like ABTS
+. Zhao et al. [51] employed garlic as precursor to develop N, S-co-doped carbon dots with good photochemical stability, biocompatibility and desirable antioxidant activity towards DPPH. Qiu et al. [52] further investigated the antioxidant chemical mechanism of graphene-based materials, i.e. graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and few-layer graphene (FLG) against various ROS, which were shown to be highly effective hydroxyl radical scavengers, and also disclosed that their antioxidant reactive sites are related with the sp2 carbon network other than oxygen-containing functional groups. Despite all the in vitro studies, carbon nanomaterials need to be further developed for in vivo studies when comes to antioxidant function. Because biology environment is filled with various RONS, more compatible with multi-enzyme like nanomaterials are urgently desired. Meanwhile, the ideal nanomaterial should have good biocompatibility in vivo, therefore the sizes, shapes and surface modification should be taken into consideration in the future. The l-QDs synthesized in this work can decompose excessive hydrogen peroxide in brain through the catalase-like activity, improve oxygen partial pressure, and have a good protective effect on oxygen-sensitive brain tissues. Moreover, the quantum dots can also eliminate a variety of free radicals, reduce oxidative stress, and inhibit neuroinflammation, providing a new way for the treatment of hypoxic brain injury.

In summary, we developed a quantum dots polymerized by l-lysine with catalase-like activity by a facile microwave-heating way. The L-QDs can decompose hydrogen peroxide to provide oxygen, contributing to improving the hypoxic circumstance in brain. Simultaneously, the L-QDs exhibit multiple free radical scavenging capacities. The survival rate of H2O2/LPS-stimulated neural cells is effectively promoted by L-QDs eliminating ROS in vitro. The oxidative stress indicators and inflammatory responses induced by hypoxic brain injury show an improvement under the L-QDs intravenous injection. In addition, motor function and spatial memory of TBI mice are also promoted to some extent by the L-QDs treatment. This work provides a new insight in the therapy of hypoxic brain injury.

L-lysine (BR, 99 %) was purchased from Tianjin Heowns Technology Co., Ltd. Potassium superoxide (KO2) and Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, AR, 30 wt%) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Technology Co., Ltd. 5-(Diethoxyphosphoryl)-5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DEPMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetra-methyl-4-piperidine(TEMP) and 5-tert-Butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (BMPO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. S-nitroso-N-acetylpenicillamine (SNAP) and 2,2Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl(DPPH, 97 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, ≥96 %), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, AR, ≥99 %) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR, 36–38 %) from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical technology Co., Ltd. 18-crown-6 and carboxy-PTIO were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The manufacturer of Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is Amresco. All solutions are prepared using ultrapure water (Millipore, 18.2 MΩ) unless otherwise specified, and none of the reagents were further purified before use.

The hydrodynamic diameter was analyzed by Zetasizer nano ZS90 DLS ((Marvin, UK). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was taken on the JEM-2100 F TEM. The infrared spectrum was measured by Fourier Transform infrared spectrometer FTIR (Nicolet Is10, America).The valence of the element was tested by PHI XPS X-Ray Photo Electron spectroscopy (XPS, Perkin Elmer, USA) with the Al Kα excitation. UV–vis spectrum is recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 3600). Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy measurements were performed using Fluorospectrophotometer (F-2500, Hitachi, Japan). Quinine sulfate in Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used as the standard substance (fluorescent quantum yield ∼ 54 %) to measure the fluorescent quantum yield of L-QDs. The solubility of oxygen was recorded by the dissolved oxygen meter (HACH LOD, American).

L-lysine (1 g) was fully dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL) in a beaker. The solution was carbonized in the microwave oven for 30 min at a constant temperature of 90℃。The reaction solution was evaporated to 4 mL at a constant temperature of 45 ℃. The reaction solution was purified by dialysis (MWCO: 0.5 kDa) for 9 times, 8 h each time, and then further purified by ultrafiltration (MWCO: 10 kDa) for 3 times. Finally the purified solution was dried in a vacuum to produce a powdered sample.

Solid DPPH was dissolved in DMSO into a 2 mM solution, then diluted with ultrapure water into a 25 μM solution. In 200 μL reaction solution, the concentration of DPPH was 25 μM and the concentration of L-QDs was in the range 0−125 μg/mL. After incubation for half an hour, the absorption value of DPPH at 340 nm was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

In the condition of ice water mixture, 10 mL H2O2 solution (50 mmol/L) and 10 mL NaNO2 solution (50 mmol/L) were mixed in a beaker and stirred continuously with glass rod for 5 min. Then, 5 mL HCl solution(1 mol/L) and 5 mL NaOH solution(1.5 mol/L) were poured into the beaker successively (about a second apart). When the solution turned yellow, ONOO− was produced. After dilution, the maximum absorption value A302 nm was found at 302 nm. Then 190 μL ONOO− was mixed with 10 μL 2 mg/mL L-QDs and A302 nm was measured with UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV3600, Shimadzu, Japan) to characterize the scavenging activities of L-QDs to ONOO−.The control group was replaced with 10 μL deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of catalase-like activity of L-QDs
ce:section.ce:para: (1) In the bubble observation photo, the concentration of hydrogen peroxide was 2 M (diluted with 10 mM PBS).and the concentration of L-QDs was 40 μg/mL in the 500 μL reaction solution. After reaction for 30 min, the photo was taken. (2) L-QDs catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide was performed in 200 μL reaction solution. The concentration of hydrogen peroxide was 30 mM (diluted with 10 mM PBS) and the concentration L-QDs was 100 μg/mL. The reduction in hydrogen peroxide absorption at 240 nm was measured by VU-vis within 20 min. L-QDs were not present in the control group. (3) Nitrogen was used to remove the dissolved oxygen in 5 mL hydrogen peroxide solution (0−1500 mM, diluted with 10 mM PBS). Then 10 μL 2 mg/mL L-QDs was added and reacted for 5 min. The change of solubility of oxygen (unit: mg/mL) in the reaction solution was recorded with dissolved oxygen meters (HACH LDO, America).

Hydroxyl radicals were generated by shining ultraviolet light on hydrogen peroxide solution (diluted with 10 mM PBS) of 5 mM for 5 min. After adding BMPO (50 mM), hydroxyl radicals were captured to produce BMPO/OH, and the signal was recorded by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker EMX plus, Germany). The equipment parameters were 0.6 mW microwave power and 1 G modulation amplitude. Continue to add L-QDs to the solution and recorded and observed the change in the signal until the signal no longer weakened or disappeared.

O2
− was produced by dissolving KO2 and 18-crown-6 in BMPO. Concentrations of KO2 and 18-crown-6 were 2.5 nM and 25 mM, respectively. After adding BMPO, O2
− was captured to produce BMPO/
OOH. When ESR was used to record signals, the group without L-QDs was used as the control group, while the group with L-QDs was used as the experimental group. According to the changes of two groups of signal characteristic peaks, the scavenging ability of L-QDs to O2
− was characterized. Parameters are set as above.

SNAP (10 μM) and carboxy-PTIO (250 μM) were completely dissolved in PBS (10 mM), and the signal of
ON could be detected by ESR (control group). In the experimental group, the change of ESR signal was recorded again after L-QDs was added. Parameters were set as above.

N2a cells were employed in all cellular experiments to evaluate cell protection of L-QDs. Cells were kept in 96-well plates filled with Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) overnight at 37 °C with 5% CO2. For cell toxicity, cell culture medium was replaced by fresh DMEM with various concentration of L-QDs and incubated for 24 h. For cell viability, cell culture medium were replaced by fresh DMEM containing H2O2 (Sigma, 88597) or LPS (Solarbio, L8880) solution (200 μM and 0.5 mg/mL respectively). Then L-QDs in DMEM with different concentration was added into the wells and incubated 24 h. Both cell toxicity and cell viability were tested by MTT assay.

N2a cells were seeded into 6-well plates with 1 × 105 cells per well. Then cells were cultured with 200 μM H2O2 or 0.5 mg/mL LPS for 24 h, supplied with 100 μg/mL L-QDs. The intracellular oxidative stress was detected by ROS sensitive fluorescent dye DCFH-DA (Sigma), which can enter into cells and glow green fluorescence after oxidation in cells. According to manufacturer’s protocols, cells were kept under 37 °C for a period of time. After that, plates were washed with PBS three times. Then, Hoechst 33342 as counterstain agent was used to stain cell nuclei in order to show numbers of cells. The fluorescent intensity was analyzed quantitatively by fluorescent microscopy and flow cytometry (FCM).

N2a cells were grown in 6-well plates to reach 70∼80 % confluence, then the L-QDs solution was added to each well. Before microscope examination, cells were washed with PBS 3 times to remove extra L-QDs, and cellular uptake was assessed at 2 h by a fluorescent microscope (EVOS, AMG).

C57 BL/6 mice (21−23 g) were purchased and maintained under the guideline approved by Key Laboratory of Post-trauma Neuro-repair and Regeneration in Central Nervous System, Tianjin Medical University General Hospital. Animals were raised under SPF level environment in 12 h dark/light cycle and supplied with water and food. The mice were anesthetized with 10 % chloral hydrate (3 mg/kg, intraperitoneal injection). Depth of anesthesia was assessed by monitoring the respiratory rate and pedal with- drawal reflexes. A 3-mm-diameter hole was drilled into the skull (2.0-mm posterior from the bregma and 2.0-mm lateral to the sagittal suture) with the dura matter intact. The injury was induced by an impactor at the velocity of 5 m/s, depth of 0.4 mm and duration of 150 ms. After injury, the scalp was sewed up. All mice were placed on a heated blanket until recovered from anesthesia. Then mice were divided into three groups (control, TBI and TBI + L-QDs). Mice were intravenously injected with L-QDs immediately after injury at the dose of 45 mg/Kg.

Brain tissue were picked up post CCI injury at different time points and homogenized for lipid peroxidation (S0131, Beyotime), H2O2 (S0038, Beyotime), SOD (S0109, Beyotime) and GSH/GSSG (S0053, Beyotime) assay. The experiments were all according to the instructions.

Brain tissue were taken after perfusion several days after CCI injury. All tissues were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde and embedded in paraffin. The slides were dewaxed inxylene, dehydrated in gradient alcohol and blocked inactivation of endogenous peroxidase before heat-mediated antigen revival. Then samples were blocked with 10 % goat serum for a period of time at 37 °C. Anti-MMP-9, anti-GFAP, Iba-1, and NeuN primary antibody were added onto each sample, and placed at 4 °C overnight. The tissues were washed in TBST for three times and incubated with secondary antibody at room temperature for 1–1.5 hours. Nuclear was stained with DAPI before slides were performed with fluorescent anti-quenching agent. Tissue apoptosis was tested using terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) assay kit (Beyotime, C1090). The procedure was based on instructions.

Morris maze test was performed in a circle water pool for five days and 4 trails/day from four different quadrants for each mouse to search the hidden platform in the middle of quadrant I without intermission. The time of each trail was 60 s. If mice succeeded to find the hidden platform within 60 s, the trail finished. Otherwise, mice would be guided to stand on the platform for 5 s to remember the site. After 5 days training, mice were placed into the water from quadrant I with platform removed from the water pool and forced to swim for 60 s. The travel path and other indicators were recorded by camera.

The experiments were independently performed multiple times (at least three times for all the data shown) to allow statistical analyses. SPSS statistical software (version 22.0, IBM) was used for all statistical analyses in the present study. All of the data were expressed as average ± standard deviations (SD). One-way ANOVA followed by multiple comparison by LSD test was used for comparisons between multiple groupsand p-values less than 0.05 were considered to be statistically signiﬁcant.

Qifeng Li, Yalong Gao, Jun shen: Carried out the experiments, Collected important background information, Performed the data analysis, Drafted the manuscript. Jianning Zhang: Supervision, Validation, Guidance. Xiaoyu Mu, Xiao-Dong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Guidance, Writing - original draft. Junying Wang, Lufei Ouyang, Ke Chen, Qinjuan Ren, Si Sun: Biological experiments, Data collection. Jiahui Pei, Haile Liu: Writing - review & editing. Hua He: Material synthesis. Yuanming Sun, Lei Zhou, Wei Long: Technical supports.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was ﬁnancially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81330029, 91859101, 81801234, 81971744, U1932107, 82001952 and 81471786) and National Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (No. 19JCZDJC34000), the Innovation Foundation of Tianjin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2021.102286.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Schneider, One-Step Synthesis of Diamine-Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots from Graphene Oxide and Their Chelating and Antioxidant Activities, Nanomaterials (Basel) 10(1) (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, M. Lan, X. Zhu, H. Xue, T.W. Ng, X. Meng, C.S. Lee, P. Wang, W. Zhang, Green Synthesis of Bifunctional Fluorescent Carbon Dots from Garlic for Cellular Imaging and Free Radical Scavenging, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 7(31) (2015) 17054-60.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antioxidant chemistry of graphene-based materials and its role in oxidation protection technology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Qiu, Z. Wang, A.C. Owens, I. Kulaots, Y. Chen, A.B. Kane, R.H. Hurt, Antioxidant chemistry of graphene-based materials and its role in oxidation protection technology, Nanoscale 6(20) (2014) 11744-55.
